JONES; Well, they give as much as they get, The dissenting opinion in Livingston points out in Employees of the government learn a lot in the The New Mexico gross receipts tax involved in this construction services. In addition, New Mexico has what's called a The use tax involved in this case is essentially a complement to the gross receipts tax to protect New Mexico Since 1819, it has been unquestionably clear that the federal government and its instrumentalities are constitutionally immune from any form of state taxation, absent congressional consent. In our system, the tax immunity of the federal government is the unavoidable consequence of the supremacy declared by the Constitution. Kern-Limerick establishes the principle that the federal government's immunity from taxation is preserved even though work through private contractors or private agents. Kern-Limerick rests very soundly on the truism that the government can only function through employees or agents. And if the individuals or entitites through whom the government works can be taxed with respect to their activities on behalf of the government, federal immunity would be largely illusory. Accepting this analysis, the court of appeals nonetheless found that the management contractors in this case are not agents for the purposes of procuring goods and services for the United States. That conclusion is contrary Court's summary affirmative decision in Livingston, the Tennessee Supreme Court's decision in United States v. Boyd, which was not -- QUESTIONi Are those entirely consistent with County of Fresno? HR. JONES: Yes. County of Fresno involved the question of the state's power to tax an employee of the government's interest, beneficial interest or beneficial use of government-owned property.
